locked_facts: Electric flux Φ = E·A cosθ where θ is angle between E and area normal
Gauss's law: total flux through closed surface Φ = q_enclosed/ε₀
Flux depends only on enclosed charge, not on shape or size of Gaussian surface
For a point charge, flux through any closed surface enclosing it = q/ε₀
If no charge is enclosed, net flux through any closed surface is zero
Gauss's law is most useful when the charge distribution has symmetry (spherical, cylindrical, planar)
truth_statements: The total electric flux through a closed surface equals the net charge enclosed divided by ε₀
Electric flux through a closed surface is independent of the shape of the surface
If the net charge enclosed by a Gaussian surface is zero, the net flux is zero even if there are external charges
Gauss's law is equivalent to Coulomb's law but is more useful for symmetric charge distributions
The flux through an open surface depends on the field and the orientation of the surface

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two differently shaped Gaussian surfaces (sphere and irregular blob) around the same point charge — both show identical flux values Φ = q/ε₀ on their readouts despite vastly different shapes
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Two surfaces, same enclosed charge, same flux number displayed on both
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students believe larger or differently shaped surfaces capture more flux — seeing identical values makes the independence from shape undeniable

simulation_states.label: Point charge with field lines
simulation_states.description: A point charge at the centre with radial field lines. No Gaussian surface yet. The number of field lines represents the charge magnitude.
simulation_states.emphasis: Field lines radiate uniformly from charge

simulation_states.label: Spherical Gaussian surface
simulation_states.description: A spherical surface appears around the charge. Flux calculation shown: Φ = q/ε₀. Each field line passing through the surface is counted.
simulation_states.emphasis: Count the field lines piercing the sphere — this number IS the flux

simulation_states.label: Two surfaces compared
simulation_states.description: A second irregular-shaped surface appears around the same charge. Both surfaces show identical Φ = q/ε₀. The key: same number of field lines pierce both surfaces.
simulation_states.emphasis: Different shapes, same enclosed charge, identical flux — shape doesn't matter

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student can move the Gaussian surface, change its shape, or move the charge outside. When charge is outside, net flux = 0 (lines enter and exit). Adjust charge magnitude to see flux scale.
simulation_states.emphasis: Move charge outside the surface — flux drops to zero even though field exists inside

common_misconceptions.belief: A larger Gaussian surface captures more flux
common_misconceptions.correction: Flux depends only on enclosed charge, not on the size or shape of the Gaussian surface
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show two surfaces of different sizes around same charge with identical flux readouts

common_misconceptions.belief: Electric flux means the electric field passes through a surface
common_misconceptions.correction: Flux is E·A cosθ — it depends on field strength, area, AND the angle between E and the surface normal
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Tilt a flat surface in a uniform field and show flux changing with angle θ
